Cea de-a șasea ediție a… Articolul Cluj-Napoca devine, din nou, capitala sportului accesibil și pentru persoanele cu dizabilități și din grupuri vulnerabile apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Republica Moldova are un nou Guvern. Cine sunt miniștrii care vor veni să cerșească bani la București
romania.summary: Republica Moldova are un nou Guvern, condus de Vasile Tofan, iar România poate să înceapă să pună bani deoparte. Noul cabinet de la Chișinău a fost învestit cu promisiuni de integrare europeană, dezvoltare economică și reforme, însă adevărata prioritate ar fi fost deja stabilită: identificarea celor mai rapide rute până la București, la cerșeală de […] The post Republica Moldova are un nou Guvern. Cine sunt miniștrii care vor veni să cerșească bani la București appeared first on Times New Roman .

În satele din Galați, Suceava, Vaslui… Articolul Peste 320 de elevi din comunitățile rurale care învață în clase simultane au beneficiat de o metodă inovatoare de predare apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: 😂 Orele de religie nu vor fi eliminate. Așa e mai ieftin decât să aduci un clovn!
romania.summary: Decizie finală la Ministerul Educației. Orele de religie vor rămâne în programa școlară și vor fi extinse la toate clasele. Din calculele făcute de Guvernul Bolojan, este mai ieftin să aduci un profesor de religie decât un clovn, un animator sau o stripteuză la clasele mai mari. La plata cu ora, este net mai avantajoasă […] The post Orele de religie nu vor fi eliminate. Așa e mai ieftin decât să aduci un clovn! appeared first on Times New Roman .

Articolul Peace, Bro! apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: Psiholog
romania.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

medical.title: Birth exposes babies to maternal gut bacteria that help build early microbiomes
medical.summary: The very first thing many babies encounter when they're born is their mother's poop. If they're born vaginally, head down, with the face against their mother's rectum, the pressure can expel fecal material just as they are born. This early exposure is not only normal but likely to be beneficial.

medical.title: Oral antiviral blocks airborne measles-like virus spread in ferret model
medical.summary: A new oral antiviral drug candidate, administered either before or after direct-contact or airborne exposure to canine distemper virus, which causes measles-like disease in ferrets, blocks virus transmission and reduces clinical symptoms, according to a study published in the journal Nature Microbiology by researchers at the Center for Translational Antiviral Research (CTAR) at Georgia State University.

medical.title: Moral distress: For primary care doctors, 'doing the right thing' can feel impossible
medical.summary: Modern health care systems ask much of primary care doctors. They are expected to diagnose, reassure, coordinate care, manage chronic illness and increasingly fill the gaps when other parts of the system come under strain.

medical.title: New wearable ring tracks glucose, ketone and other biomarkers in sweat simultaneously
medical.summary: Engineers at the University of California San Diego created a smart ring that simultaneously and continuously monitors up to four different chemical biomarkers from finger sweat. The biomarkers the smart ring can monitor in sweat are glucose, ketones, vitamin C, uric acid, lactate and alcohol.

medical.title: The low‑tech fix that will reduce brain injuries in sport
medical.summary: For parents around the world, weekend mornings often look the same: standing on the sidelines of a soccer field, basketball court or American football field. In Australia's southern states, it's often an Australian rules football oval. But a few weeks ago, tragedy struck when footballer Nathan Fitzgerald died after sustaining three blows to the head during a game.

medical.title: Could this enzyme help remove 'zombie' cells from our tissues?
medical.summary: Our population is aging. The United Nations reports that by 2050, the number of people age 85 and older on our planet is set to triple. But a longer life span isn't the same as a longer health span (the number of years we spend healthy). Salk Institute scientists are working to change that through foundational research that explores the cellular mechanisms driving age-related dysfunction.

medical.title: AI helps Stanford scientists discover “natural Ozempic” without the usual side effects
medical.summary: Stanford Medicine researchers have discovered a naturally occurring molecule that may suppress appetite and reduce body weight much like Ozempic, but without several of its common side effects. The molecule, called BRP, acts on a more targeted region of the brain involved in hunger and metabolism rather than affecting tissues throughout the body.

medical.title: Is it worse to eat unhealthy food in the morning, or later in the day? And what if you skip breakfast?
medical.summary: Many of us have grown up with the mantra that breakfast is the most important meal of the day. But whether it's snatching an extra half hour of sleep or focusing on getting the kids out of the house, there are various reasons why assembling a healthy breakfast might slip down your list of priorities—especially on a weekday.

medical.title: Scientists discover a compound that could supercharge aging muscle repair
medical.summary: A sulfur-based compound called LASSS appears to protect and supercharge a key protein involved in repairing damaged muscle. The discovery could eventually lead to new ways to slow muscle loss and preserve strength as people age.

medical.title: A new antiviral could stop measles before an outbreak takes off
medical.summary: An experimental pill prevented a measles-like virus from spreading through the air or through close contact in ferrets. It worked when given shortly before or after exposure and also shortened illness and the period when infected animals remained contagious. Researchers say the drug could one day complement vaccination and make outbreaks easier to contain.

medical.title: Clinical trial finds low-dose olanzapine reduces nausea after surgery
medical.summary: Many people feel that the worst part of surgery happens after they wake up. Countless patients who undergo general anesthesia worry about nausea and vomiting, common side effects after surgery. A clinical trial by Yale School of Medicine's Department of Anesthesiology finds that a small dose of olanzapine, an inexpensive antipsychotic medication, could curb those symptoms. The treatment could also ensure a smoother postsurgical recovery.

medical.title: Mental disorders surge among young adults
medical.summary: Mental disorders among young adults have surged, with new research showing the proportion of young Australians meeting diagnostic criteria for anxiety, mood or substance disorders increased from 23.4% to 36.2% from 2007 to 2022.

medical.title: Ozempic and Mounjaro linked to a surprising hair loss risk
medical.summary: A large study suggests that GLP-1 drugs including Ozempic and Mounjaro may slightly increase the risk of hair loss in people with type 2 diabetes. Compared with two other common classes of diabetes medications, GLP-1 users had a significantly higher rate of alopecia, though the absolute risk was still low. The hair loss was mainly non-scarring, which means it may be reversible.

medical.title: Major genetic breakthrough reveals hidden causes of severe morning sickness in pregnancy
medical.summary: A massive genetic study has identified nine new genes linked to hyperemesis gravidarum, the most severe form of pregnancy sickness, bringing the total number of known risk genes to ten. The discoveries provide fresh insight into why some women experience debilitating nausea and vomiting during pregnancy while others do not. Several of the newly identified genes are involved in appetite, nausea, metabolism, and brain function, opening the door to more targeted treatments.

science.title: Why you should think twice before rescuing a wild animal
science.summary: Imagine you're driving down a winding road when you spot an injured possum. Your instincts might tell you to hit the brakes, check what's wrong and bundle it into your car. Research shows many of us want to help wild animals in distress. This may be out of empathy, obligation or simply wanting to do the right thing.

science.title: Combining AI protein design with laboratory evolution improves engineered enzymes
science.summary: When scientists want to create a protein that can potentially treat disease, one approach is to begin with a different protein and evolve it in the lab, selecting over many generations for versions with a new, desired function.

science.title: 'Jimothy' the raccoon has short spine syndrome—but this condition can affect dogs, too
science.summary: If you've been anywhere near the internet in recent days, you will likely have seen the viral footage of "Jimothy": an unusually round raccoon in Seattle that hops and skips along in a video filmed by local resident Kiana Hall.

science.title: Oxygen collisions at the LHC show new indications of extreme state of matter
science.summary: All four main LHC experiments have found new signs that oxygen and neon collisions may create the extreme state of matter that existed during the first microseconds after the Big Bang.

science.title: Fossilized wood reveals 300 million years of Earth's history
science.summary: Many people are familiar with fossilized wood as a decorative item from a museum shop. However, the fact that it can preserve the geological history of entire regions spanning millions of years is a new discovery. A research team led by geologist Dr. Steffen Trümper from the University of Münster has now demonstrated for the first time that fossilized wood is a natural archive from which the geological history of an entire region over hundreds of millions of years can be deduced.

science.title: Plumage reflectance framework offers new insight into how birds evolved heat-controlling feathers
science.summary: Birds can inhabit various environments with different climates and temperatures, ranging from cold polar regions to scorching hot deserts. To survive and thrive in these very different environments, they need to effectively regulate their body temperature.

science.title: Rejection hurts: How to help kids and teens bounce back when they've missed out
science.summary: Rejection can sometimes be so intense it feels like physical pain—a punch in the gut or a heart that aches. It often involves feeling left out and unwanted. And it can make us question our self-worth.

science.title: World's first 'zinc oxide spin qubit' could advance scalable quantum devices
science.summary: A research team led by SKKU professor Hosung Seo of the Department of Quantum Information Engineering and the SKKU Advanced Institute of Nanotechnology, working with the University of Wisconsin–Madison and the University of Washington, has identified—for the first time—an atomic defect structure in the zinc oxide (ZnO) semiconductor with outstanding properties for use as a "spin qubit," a core building block of future quantum computers, quantum communications and quantum sensors.

science.title: Differences in human and great ape laughter reveal clues to the origin of speech
science.summary: Humans are not the only animals that communicate. Birds sing to attract mates, dolphins use signature whistles that identify individuals, and many primates produce complex vocalizations that help them coordinate their social lives.

science.title: Physicists turn to the universe's 'piano notes' to detect hidden particles
science.summary: It's been said that a finely tuned ear knows the size and shape of a piano by merely listening to the instrument's notes. An international team of physicists has now devised an analogous approach to detect the universe's hidden particles at high energies—opening a potential pathway for discovering new laws of physics.

science.title: A simple idea to bring clean drinking water to U.S. homes at a lower cost
science.summary: Stanford engineer Khalid Osman and collaborators are building modeling tools to help water system managers find the most cost-effective option for improving their community's drinking water.

science.title: Astronomers catch massive galaxy assembling piece by piece 1.2 billion years after Big Bang
science.summary: Astronomers have discovered a remarkably tiny group of six young galaxies just 1.2 billion years after the Big Bang. This may be a rare glimpse of how some of the universe's largest galaxies formed. The paper outlining the findings was submitted to the arXiv preprint server on July 13.

science.title: Vegan dog and cat food generally well digested
science.summary: A new scientific review has found that nutritionally sound vegan dog food, vegan cat food and vegetarian pet diets are generally well digested and broadly comparable to conventional meat-based diets.

science.title: Hubble Spies One-Sided Spiral
science.summary: This Hubble image features spiral galaxy NGC 4654, located 72 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo (the Maiden).

science.title: Sweden’s hidden rare earth deposits could help break China’s grip
science.summary: Sweden could become an important source of rare earth elements needed for magnets and green technologies. Instead of extracting one target metal, researchers are cataloging everything contained in Swedish mineral deposits. They will then develop magnets using those naturally available combinations, potentially reducing pollution, waste, and reliance on China.

science.title: China’s Tianwen-1 captures interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS near Mars
science.summary: China’s Tianwen-1 orbiter captured a faint but remarkable view of 3I/ATLAS as the interstellar comet raced past Mars. The images reveal its tail and glowing coma, offering scientists a glimpse of material born beyond our solar system.

science.title: Ancient ape fossil in Egypt challenges the story of human origins
science.summary: A newly identified fossil ape from northern Egypt is reshaping ideas about where the ancestors of modern apes first emerged. Analyses place it closer to living apes than any known East African species of the same age, pointing to North Africa and the Middle East as a possible birthplace and crossroads for the ape lineage that eventually produced gibbons, orangutans, gorillas, chimpanzees, and humans.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Schimbarea educației din România de jos în sus, prin pregătirea generației a 13-a de profesori-soluție pentru școli din mediul rural.
mix_items.summary: București, 17 iulie 2026 – Teach for Romania a dat startul Academiei de Leadership și Formare în Educație, programul intensiv prin care pregătește cea de-a 13-a generație de viitori lideri în educație – directori, profesori, învățători și educatori, care să predea, începând cu anul școlar 2026-2027, și să transforme educația de la firul ierbii, în […]

mix_items.title: Aproape 100.000 de copii învață în clase simultane. World Vision România propune o soluție națională pentru profesorii care predau mai multor clase în aceeași oră.
mix_items.summary: București, 16 iulie. Aproape 100.000 de elevi din România învață în clase organizate în regim simultan, majoritatea în mediul rural,... mix_items.title: Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Descoperă povestea Alinei și cum sprijinul oferit de Fundația Inocenți a ajutat-o să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile școlare și curajul de a reuși. The post Inocenți, locul în care curajul crește pas cu pas appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Părinte Social (Mamă SOS) – București și Cisnădie, Sibiu Transformă un loc de muncă într-o misiune de viață La SOS Satele Copiilor România, credem că fiecare copil are dreptul să... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Why you should think twice before rescuing a wild animal
mix_items.summary: Imagine you're driving down a winding road when you spot an injured possum. Your instincts might tell you to hit the brakes, check what's wrong and bundle it into your car. Research shows many of us want to help wild animals in distress. This may be out of empathy, obligation or simply wanting to do the right thing.

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Birth exposes babies to maternal gut bacteria that help build early microbiomes
mix_items.summary: The very first thing many babies encounter when they're born is their mother's poop. If they're born vaginally, head down, with the face against their mother's rectum, the pressure can expel fecal material just as they are born. This early exposure is not only normal but likely to be beneficial.

mix_items.title: Oral antiviral blocks airborne measles-like virus spread in ferret model
mix_items.summary: A new oral antiviral drug candidate, administered either before or after direct-contact or airborne exposure to canine distemper virus, which causes measles-like disease in ferrets, blocks virus transmission and reduces clinical symptoms, according to a study published in the journal Nature Microbiology by researchers at the Center for Translational Antiviral Research (CTAR) at Georgia State University.

mix_items.title: Combining AI protein design with laboratory evolution improves engineered enzymes
mix_items.summary: When scientists want to create a protein that can potentially treat disease, one approach is to begin with a different protein and evolve it in the lab, selecting over many generations for versions with a new, desired function.

mix_items.title: Moral distress: For primary care doctors, 'doing the right thing' can feel impossible
mix_items.summary: Modern health care systems ask much of primary care doctors. They are expected to diagnose, reassure, coordinate care, manage chronic illness and increasingly fill the gaps when other parts of the system come under strain.

mix_items.title: New wearable ring tracks glucose, ketone and other biomarkers in sweat simultaneously
mix_items.summary: Engineers at the University of California San Diego created a smart ring that simultaneously and continuously monitors up to four different chemical biomarkers from finger sweat. The biomarkers the smart ring can monitor in sweat are glucose, ketones, vitamin C, uric acid, lactate and alcohol.

mix_items.title: 'Jimothy' the raccoon has short spine syndrome—but this condition can affect dogs, too
mix_items.summary: If you've been anywhere near the internet in recent days, you will likely have seen the viral footage of "Jimothy": an unusually round raccoon in Seattle that hops and skips along in a video filmed by local resident Kiana Hall.

mix_items.title: Oxygen collisions at the LHC show new indications of extreme state of matter
mix_items.summary: All four main LHC experiments have found new signs that oxygen and neon collisions may create the extreme state of matter that existed during the first microseconds after the Big Bang.

mix_items.title: Fossilized wood reveals 300 million years of Earth's history
mix_items.summary: Many people are familiar with fossilized wood as a decorative item from a museum shop. However, the fact that it can preserve the geological history of entire regions spanning millions of years is a new discovery. A research team led by geologist Dr. Steffen Trümper from the University of Münster has now demonstrated for the first time that fossilized wood is a natural archive from which the geological history of an entire region over hundreds of millions of years can be deduced.

mix_items.title: The low‑tech fix that will reduce brain injuries in sport
mix_items.summary: For parents around the world, weekend mornings often look the same: standing on the sidelines of a soccer field, basketball court or American football field. In Australia's southern states, it's often an Australian rules football oval. But a few weeks ago, tragedy struck when footballer Nathan Fitzgerald died after sustaining three blows to the head during a game.

mix_items.title: Could this enzyme help remove 'zombie' cells from our tissues?
mix_items.summary: Our population is aging. The United Nations reports that by 2050, the number of people age 85 and older on our planet is set to triple. But a longer life span isn't the same as a longer health span (the number of years we spend healthy). Salk Institute scientists are working to change that through foundational research that explores the cellular mechanisms driving age-related dysfunction.

mix_items.title: AI helps Stanford scientists discover “natural Ozempic” without the usual side effects
mix_items.summary: Stanford Medicine researchers have discovered a naturally occurring molecule that may suppress appetite and reduce body weight much like Ozempic, but without several of its common side effects. The molecule, called BRP, acts on a more targeted region of the brain involved in hunger and metabolism rather than affecting tissues throughout the body.

mix_items.title: Plumage reflectance framework offers new insight into how birds evolved heat-controlling feathers
mix_items.summary: Birds can inhabit various environments with different climates and temperatures, ranging from cold polar regions to scorching hot deserts. To survive and thrive in these very different environments, they need to effectively regulate their body temperature.

mix_items.title: Is it worse to eat unhealthy food in the morning, or later in the day? And what if you skip breakfast?
mix_items.summary: Many of us have grown up with the mantra that breakfast is the most important meal of the day. But whether it's snatching an extra half hour of sleep or focusing on getting the kids out of the house, there are various reasons why assembling a healthy breakfast might slip down your list of priorities—especially on a weekday.

mix_items.title: Rejection hurts: How to help kids and teens bounce back when they've missed out
mix_items.summary: Rejection can sometimes be so intense it feels like physical pain—a punch in the gut or a heart that aches. It often involves feeling left out and unwanted. And it can make us question our self-worth.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a compound that could supercharge aging muscle repair
mix_items.summary: A sulfur-based compound called LASSS appears to protect and supercharge a key protein involved in repairing damaged muscle. The discovery could eventually lead to new ways to slow muscle loss and preserve strength as people age.

mix_items.title: A new antiviral could stop measles before an outbreak takes off
mix_items.summary: An experimental pill prevented a measles-like virus from spreading through the air or through close contact in ferrets. It worked when given shortly before or after exposure and also shortened illness and the period when infected animals remained contagious. Researchers say the drug could one day complement vaccination and make outbreaks easier to contain.

mix_items.title: World's first 'zinc oxide spin qubit' could advance scalable quantum devices
mix_items.summary: A research team led by SKKU professor Hosung Seo of the Department of Quantum Information Engineering and the SKKU Advanced Institute of Nanotechnology, working with the University of Wisconsin–Madison and the University of Washington, has identified—for the first time—an atomic defect structure in the zinc oxide (ZnO) semiconductor with outstanding properties for use as a "spin qubit," a core building block of future quantum computers, quantum communications and quantum sensors.

mix_items.title: Differences in human and great ape laughter reveal clues to the origin of speech
mix_items.summary: Humans are not the only animals that communicate. Birds sing to attract mates, dolphins use signature whistles that identify individuals, and many primates produce complex vocalizations that help them coordinate their social lives.

mix_items.title: Physicists turn to the universe's 'piano notes' to detect hidden particles
mix_items.summary: It's been said that a finely tuned ear knows the size and shape of a piano by merely listening to the instrument's notes. An international team of physicists has now devised an analogous approach to detect the universe's hidden particles at high energies—opening a potential pathway for discovering new laws of physics.

mix_items.title: Clinical trial finds low-dose olanzapine reduces nausea after surgery
mix_items.summary: Many people feel that the worst part of surgery happens after they wake up. Countless patients who undergo general anesthesia worry about nausea and vomiting, common side effects after surgery. A clinical trial by Yale School of Medicine's Department of Anesthesiology finds that a small dose of olanzapine, an inexpensive antipsychotic medication, could curb those symptoms. The treatment could also ensure a smoother postsurgical recovery.

mix_items.title: Mental disorders surge among young adults
mix_items.summary: Mental disorders among young adults have surged, with new research showing the proportion of young Australians meeting diagnostic criteria for anxiety, mood or substance disorders increased from 23.4% to 36.2% from 2007 to 2022.

mix_items.title: A simple idea to bring clean drinking water to U.S. homes at a lower cost
mix_items.summary: Stanford engineer Khalid Osman and collaborators are building modeling tools to help water system managers find the most cost-effective option for improving their community's drinking water.

mix_items.title: Astronomers catch massive galaxy assembling piece by piece 1.2 billion years after Big Bang
mix_items.summary: Astronomers have discovered a remarkably tiny group of six young galaxies just 1.2 billion years after the Big Bang. This may be a rare glimpse of how some of the universe's largest galaxies formed. The paper outlining the findings was submitted to the arXiv preprint server on July 13.

mix_items.title: Vegan dog and cat food generally well digested
mix_items.summary: A new scientific review has found that nutritionally sound vegan dog food, vegan cat food and vegetarian pet diets are generally well digested and broadly comparable to conventional meat-based diets.

mix_items.title: Hubble Spies One-Sided Spiral
mix_items.summary: This Hubble image features spiral galaxy NGC 4654, located 72 million light-years away in the constellation Virgo (the Maiden).

mix_items.title: Sweden’s hidden rare earth deposits could help break China’s grip
mix_items.summary: Sweden could become an important source of rare earth elements needed for magnets and green technologies. Instead of extracting one target metal, researchers are cataloging everything contained in Swedish mineral deposits. They will then develop magnets using those naturally available combinations, potentially reducing pollution, waste, and reliance on China.

mix_items.title: China’s Tianwen-1 captures interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS near Mars
mix_items.summary: China’s Tianwen-1 orbiter captured a faint but remarkable view of 3I/ATLAS as the interstellar comet raced past Mars. The images reveal its tail and glowing coma, offering scientists a glimpse of material born beyond our solar system.

mix_items.title: Ancient ape fossil in Egypt challenges the story of human origins
mix_items.summary: A newly identified fossil ape from northern Egypt is reshaping ideas about where the ancestors of modern apes first emerged. Analyses place it closer to living apes than any known East African species of the same age, pointing to North Africa and the Middle East as a possible birthplace and crossroads for the ape lineage that eventually produced gibbons, orangutans, gorillas, chimpanzees, and humans.

mix_items.title: Ozempic and Mounjaro linked to a surprising hair loss risk
mix_items.summary: A large study suggests that GLP-1 drugs including Ozempic and Mounjaro may slightly increase the risk of hair loss in people with type 2 diabetes. Compared with two other common classes of diabetes medications, GLP-1 users had a significantly higher rate of alopecia, though the absolute risk was still low. The hair loss was mainly non-scarring, which means it may be reversible.

mix_items.title: Major genetic breakthrough reveals hidden causes of severe morning sickness in pregnancy
mix_items.summary: A massive genetic study has identified nine new genes linked to hyperemesis gravidarum, the most severe form of pregnancy sickness, bringing the total number of known risk genes to ten. The discoveries provide fresh insight into why some women experience debilitating nausea and vomiting during pregnancy while others do not. Several of the newly identified genes are involved in appetite, nausea, metabolism, and brain function, opening the door to more targeted treatments.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am facut buget pe luna asta. Primele doua zile au fost impecabile.

satire_ro.title: Confuzie. Nicușor Dan a primit-o la Cotroceni pe Floricica Dansatoarea în locul președintei Indiei
